I'd like to avoid having to modify the pytest arguments in `tasks.py` (i.e. by changing test targets from `'tests'` to `'tests/test_*'`) and relying on conditionally importing the test function into a non-"test_" prefixed file (`fixtures.py`).

Since the annotations test is very minor, I'd prefer to add it in a way that requires the fewest changes even if the solution is a hack (which it will be :smile:):

```python
# test_annotations.py

import pytest

import pydash as _
from pydash._compat import PY3


if PY3:
    # Hack around the fact that we can't define this kind of function in
    # Python 2 so have to rely on conditionally creating it using exec()
    # to avoid syntax errors while still having this test case covered for
    # Python 3.
    exec('def typed_function(row: int, index: int, col: list): return row + 1')
else:
    typed_function = None


@pytest.mark.skipif(not PY3, reason='test requires Python 3 annotations')
def test_annotated_callback():
    assert _.map_([1, 2], typed_function) == [2, 3]
```

It's not pretty but I think it'll do for now.

Whether an interaction is played or not is knowledge limited/known only for one use of the cassette. When you load a cassette, they're all available in that they can all be replayed. As you play some of them, they're moved to the `played` deque because they're no longer available to be played -- you don't want to allow duplicate plays of the same interaction. During that session, if you record more interactions, they're added to the `recorded` deque because they're also not available to be replayed -- you don't want to prevent someone from making a request twice if they need to, especially if they're testing some retry logic. When you save the cassette, you want all these interactions to be saved--those that were played, those that were not played, and those that were recorded. After that, everything is available because that session with the cassette is done.

We need a place to put code that puts everything together and actually runs something.  The `training` module is _given_ a dataset, model, and so on, do the training loop.  This piece of code says, build all of the prerequisites, load data, do whatever, _then_ call stuff in `training`.  Other, similar pieces of code will build prerequisites for some other task, like loading a saved model and a test dataset, then evaluating the model.

My big question is how should we structure this code?  There are a few options:

1. We could have a bunch of scripts like this in some directory, not sure where (`allennlp/experiments/`?  `allennlp/drivers/`?).  These have code to actually run the operations you want, and get called by some CLI somewhere.

2. We could use a `Driver` abstraction, like I originally had this PR, and have each of these be a class instead of a script, with a `run()` method that does it, and a unified CLI into it.

3. DeepQA had a python file called `deep_qa/run.py` that had the top-level training and evaluation entry points, and that's where this particular file pattern came from.  We could keep this general idea, but change the name of the file...

4. Some other option?

I'm really not sure what the best way to go is.  Any thoughts?

```python
def import_block(self, block):
    ...
    if block.transactions:
        apply_transaction_fns = tuple(
            functools.partial(self.apply_transaction, transaction=transaction)
            for transaction in block.transactions
        )
        headers, receipts = zip(*tuple(accumulate(
            _accumulate_apply_txn_fn, 
            apply_transaction_fns,
            (self.block.header, None),
        ))[1:])
        last_header = headers[-1]
    else:
        last_header = self.block.header
        receipts = tuple()
     ...
```

This is a new function I added.  I thought there was unnecessary code duplication, e.g. between `validate_file` and `validate_string`.  A common way to design code that processes data in such a way that it doesn't care where the data came from, is to design it to read/write from/to a stream.  I think there was a part of the design here that didn't need to care where the data came from.  That also gives flexibility to users: they can choose where the data comes from, rather than relying on us to provide a bunch of source-specific functions (e.g. validate_file, validate_string, validate_from_network, validate_from_bytebuffer, etc).  There can be one central validation function (for JSON data that isn't already parsed anyway; I think we need an analogously behaving function for parsed JSON), and source-specific validation functions can just be convenience wrappers around that.  I could cut almost all the code from `validate_string` since it's a very simple wrapper now.

I think this is the actual API surface, with `ascii` as a stand in for an arbitrary non-UTF-8 encoding:

| `text`                       | `subencoding`       | `quoted_bytes`           | return value                 |
|------------------------------|---------------------|--------------------------|------------------------------|
| `<unicode>`                  | `"utf-8"` (default) | `<bytes, UTF-8 encoded>` | `<unicode, percent-encoded>` |
| `<unicode>`                  | `"ascii"`           | `UnicodeEncodeError`     | `<unicode, unaltered>`       |
| `<unicode, ASCII congruent>` | `"ascii"`           | `<bytes, ASCII>`         | `<bytes, ASCII>`             |
| `<unicode, charmap?>`        | `False`             | `<bytes, UTF-8 encoded>` | `<bytes, UTF-8 encoded>`     |

The last case seems weird - I want `bytes` back because I know there isn't actually a "subencoding" (and I ended up with `unicode` presumably because somewhere else there's something like `.decode("charmap")`), but instead I get UTF-8 encoded bytes back.  What then is the purpose of `subencoding=False` if it always implies UTF-8?

I'm not sure how committed we are to a particular logical structure for the README files, but personally as a relative newcomer I think I'd find this content a bit easier to digest if the first two sections here were swapped, so that first we get an explanation of what the dashboard does at a very high level - the "Overview" below - first, and then we delve into the problem that it solves.

Even though the "problem" part motivates the dashboard's existence and hence comes first in a causal sense, I think as a reader it's a little easier to get a first impression of what functionality the dashboard provides first, and then read about the motivation for it.  Especially since the latter may actually require more context.  E.g. in this case, IMO it's fairly straightforward to understand the Overview since it's just "custom line charts of scalar data" which I can understand without any prior TensorBoard exposure.  In contrast, the "problem solved" section requires more awareness of the limitations of the original scalar dashboard, the structure of tag names, the notion of the viewport, etc.  So it's helpful going into that to at least know exactly what this dashboard does do.

Just my $0.02 though.

How is it only possible when all nodes are stopped? And do you mean all other non-sniffer nodes? For my implementation, the `recv` blocks until there is any data to return or it is closed. So if all nodes are stopped then no more data would be incoming, so the only thing that would cause the function to return is to close it. But since it waits for this thread to complete before closing it, there is a deadlock.

The clean up design could be something like:

```
self._thread_alive.set()
self._sniffer.close()
self._thread.join()
```

Here is the real meat of the issue... On macOS and Linux it works great to let `list2cmdline` deal with the escaping (as it should). 

```python
return shlex.split(cmd) + ["--input-title-format", self.title] + shlex.split(args)
```

But on Windows we don't want to apply `shlex.split`, as it doesn't work quite right. 

Doing this works for me on Windows, Linux and macOS. 
```python
args = self.args.format(filename=filename)
cmd = self.cmd
extra_args = []

if self.title is not None:
    # vlc
    if self.cmd in SUPPORTED_PLAYERS['vlc']:
        # see https://wiki.videolan.org/Documentation:Format_String/, allow escaping with \$
        self.title.replace("$", "$$").replace("\$$", "$")
        extra_args.extend(["--input-title-format", self.title])

    # mpv
    if self.cmd in SUPPORTED_PLAYERS['mpv']:
        # see https://mpv.io/manual/stable/#property-expansion, allow escaping with \$
        self.title.replace("$", "$$").replace("\$$", "$")
        extra_args.extend(["--title", self.title])

# other player
if is_win32:
    return cmd + " " + subprocess.list2cmdline(extra_args) + " " + args
return shlex.split(cmd) + extra_args + shlex.split(args)
```
